content:
# Joshua Chapter 7 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 230-231 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 0170-0171 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `JOS_007_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_007_page_002.jpg`; `JOS_007_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_007_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Trespass of Achan (vv. 1-5)

**1** But the sons of Israel committed a trespass regarding the devoted things, for Achan the son of Carmi, the son of Zabdi, the son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, took from the devoted things. And the anger of YHWH burned against the sons of Israel.

**2** And Joshua sent men from Jericho to Ai, which is near Beth-aven, east of Bethel, and he spoke to them, saying, "Go up and spy out the land." And the men went up and spied out Ai.

**3** And they returned to Joshua and said to him, "Let not all the people go up; let about two thousand men or about three thousand men go up and strike Ai. Do not weary all the people there, for they are few."

**4** So about three thousand men from the people went up there, and they fled before the men of Ai.

**5** And the men of Ai struck down from them about thirty-six men, and they pursued them from before the gate as far as Shebarim, and struck them down on the descent. And the heart of the people melted and became as water.

---

### Joshua's Lament before YHWH (vv. 6-9)

**6** And Joshua tore his garments and fell on his face to the earth before the ark of YHWH until the evening, he and the elders of Israel, and they put dust on their heads.

**7** And Joshua said, "Alas, Lord YHWH, why have you brought this people across the Jordan at all, to give us into the hand of the Amorites, to destroy us? Would that we had been content to dwell beyond the Jordan!

**8** Please, O Lord, what shall I say, after Israel has turned its back before its enemies?

**9** And the Canaanites and all the inhabitants of the land will hear and will surround us, and will cut off our name from the earth. And what will you do for your great name?"

---

### YHWH's Response and Command (vv. 10-15)

**10** And YHWH said to Joshua, "Rise up! Why is it that you have fallen on your face?

**11** Israel has sinned, and they have also transgressed my covenant which I commanded them, and they have also taken from the devoted things, and have also stolen, and have also deceived, and have also put them among their own vessels.

**12** And the sons of Israel are not able to stand before their enemies; they turn their backs before their enemies, for they have become a devoted thing. I will not be with you anymore, unless you destroy the devoted thing from your midst.

**13** Rise, consecrate the people, and say, 'Consecrate yourselves for tomorrow, for thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: There is a devoted thing in your midst, O Israel. You are not able to stand before your enemies until you remove the devoted thing from your midst.

**14** And in the morning you shall be brought near by your tribes. And it shall be that the tribe which YHWH takes shall come near by clans. And the clan which YHWH takes shall come near by households. And the household which YHWH takes shall come near man by man.

**15** And it shall be that the one taken with the devoted thing shall be burned with fire, he and all that belongs to him, because he has transgressed the covenant of YHWH and because he has done a disgraceful thing in Israel.'"

---

### The Selection of Achan (vv. 16-21)

**16** And Joshua rose early in the morning and brought Israel near by its tribes, and the tribe of Judah was taken.

**17** And he brought near the clan of Judah, and he took the clan of the Zerahites. And he brought near the clan of the Zerahites man by man, and Zabdi was taken.

**18** And he brought near his household man by man, and Achan the son of Carmi, the son of Zabdi, the son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, was taken.

**19** And Joshua said to Achan, "My son, give glory, I pray, to YHWH, the God of Israel, and give him a confession. Tell me now what you have done; do not hide it from me."

**20** And Achan answered Joshua and said, "Truly, I have sinned against YHWH, the God of Israel, and this is what I have done:

**21** When I saw among the spoil a beautiful mantle of Shinar, and two hundred shekels of silver, and one bar of gold, its weight fifty shekels, I coveted them and I took them. And behold, they are hidden in the earth in the midst of my tent, with the silver underneath it."

---

### The Recovery of the Stolen Goods (vv. 22-23)

**22** And Joshua sent messengers, and they ran to the tent, and behold, it was hidden in his tent, with the silver underneath it.

**23** And they took them from the midst of the tent and brought them to Joshua and to all the sons of Israel, and they spread them out before YHWH.

---

### The Execution of Judgment (vv. 24-26)

**24** And Joshua, and all Israel with him, took Achan the son of Zerah, and the silver, and the mantle, and the bar of gold, and his sons, and his daughters, and his ox, and his donkey, and his flock, and his tent, and all that belonged to him, and they brought them up to the Valley of Achor.

**25** And Joshua said, "Why have you troubled us? YHWH will trouble you this day." And all Israel stoned him with stones, and they burned them with fire, and they stoned them with stones.

**26** And they raised over him a great heap of stones, unto this day. And YHWH turned from the burning of his anger. Therefore the name of that place has been called the Valley of Achor unto this day.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **Verse 1 -- "committed a trespass" (וַיִּמְעֲלוּ...מַעַל):** The Hebrew employs the cognate accusative construction ma'al ma'al, literally "trespassed a trespass," an intensifying figure. The root מעל denotes a breach of faith or sacrilege, particularly in the context of holy things. The LXX renders επλημμελησαν πλημμελειαν μεγαλην (they committed a great offense), adding μεγαλην (great) as an interpretive expansion. The formal rendering retains "committed a trespass" to preserve the sacral weight of ma'al without the awkwardness of the literal cognate accusative.

2. **Verse 1 -- "the devoted things" (הַחֵרֶם):** The Hebrew cherem refers to that which has been irrevocably dedicated to YHWH, typically by total destruction. Jericho and its contents were placed under cherem (6:17-18). Achan's violation was not mere theft but sacrilege -- the appropriation of what belonged exclusively to YHWH. The LXX renders αναθεματος (of the anathema/devoted thing). The LOGOS rendering consistently uses "devoted thing(s)" throughout the chapter to preserve the technical covenantal meaning.

3. **Verse 1 -- Genealogy of Achan:** The MT reads עָכָן (Achan). The LXX reads Αχαρ (Achar), which is also the form in 1 Chronicles 2:7, where the name is given an explicit etymology: "Achar, the troubler of Israel." The Leningrad Codex reads Achan consistently; the alternate form Achar may reflect a deliberate wordplay on the verb עכר (to trouble), which appears in verse 25 and in the name of the Valley of Achor. The formal rendering follows the MT vocalization.

4. **Verse 2 -- "Ai" (הָעַי):** The Hebrew has the definite article: ha-Ai, "the Ai" (literally "the ruin"). The name itself means "ruin" or "heap," and the article suggests it functions as a descriptive designation rather than a proper name. The LXX renders Γαι, without the article. The formal rendering uses "Ai" without the article, following English convention, but notes the Hebrew morphology.

5. **Verse 2 -- "Beth-aven" (בֵּית אָוֶן):** Literally "house of wickedness" or "house of nothingness." This toponym appears as a genuine place name here and in 1 Samuel 13:5; 14:23, but in Hosea 4:15; 5:8; 10:5 it is used as a derogatory pun for Bethel ("house of God" becoming "house of nothingness") after the establishment of the golden calf shrine. The LXX renders Βηθων. The formal rendering transliterates the name.

6. **Verse 5 -- "Shebarim" (הַשְּׁבָרִים):** This term, with the definite article, may be a proper place name or a common noun meaning "the broken places" or "the quarries," from the root שׁבר (to break). The LXX renders συντριψαν αυτους (they crushed them), apparently reading it as a verbal form rather than a place name. The formal rendering follows the MT in treating it as a toponym, consistent with the geographical specificity of the surrounding context (the gate, the descent).

7. **Verse 5 -- "the heart of the people melted and became as water" (וַיִּמַּס לְבַב־הָעָם וַיְהִי לְמָיִם):** This is a deliberate reversal of 5:1, where the hearts of the Canaanite kings "melted" (the same root מסס) upon hearing of Israel's crossing. Israel's disobedience has transferred their enemies' terror to themselves. The LXX renders και επτοηθη η καρδια του λαου και εγενετο ωσπερ υδωρ (and the heart of the people was terrified and became as water). The narrative irony is structural and would have been unmistakable to the original audience.

8. **Verse 7 -- "Alas, Lord YHWH" (אֲהָהּ אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה):** The interjection אֲהָהּ expresses anguished lament. The combination Adonai YHWH (Lord YHWH) is the solemn address form used in prophetic lament (cf. Jeremiah 1:6; 4:10; 14:13; 32:17; Ezekiel 4:14; 9:8; 11:13). The LXX renders δεομαι κυριε (I beseech, O Lord). Joshua's prayer echoes the Israelites' complaint in the wilderness (Exodus 14:11-12; Numbers 14:2-3), casting him momentarily in the role of a despairing murmurer rather than a confident leader -- a characterization YHWH will sharply correct in verse 10.

9. **Verse 9 -- "what will you do for your great name?" (וּמַה־תַּעֲשֵׂה לִשְׁמְךָ הַגָּדוֹל):** Joshua's argument shifts from despair to theology: YHWH's reputation among the nations is at stake. This "name theology" appeal -- arguing that YHWH must act to preserve his own honor -- has deep roots in the Mosaic tradition (Exodus 32:12; Numbers 14:13-16; Deuteronomy 9:28). The LXX renders και τι ποιησεις το ονομα σου το μεγα. The appeal assumes that YHWH's identity is bound to Israel's fate in the sight of the nations.

10. **Verse 11 -- Fivefold accusation:** YHWH's response contains a devastating chain of five verbs connected by וְגַם (and also): "sinned...transgressed...taken...stolen...deceived." The repetition of "and also" (vegam) creates a rhythmic accumulation of guilt. The LXX follows the same structure with και...και...και. The formal rendering preserves each "and also" to reflect the rhetorical force of the Hebrew.

11. **Verse 12 -- "for they have become a devoted thing" (כִּי הָיוּ לְחֵרֶם):** This is the theological crux of the chapter. Because Israel has taken from the cherem, Israel itself has become cherem -- subject to the same total destruction that was decreed upon Jericho. The contagion of the devoted thing has spread from the stolen objects to the entire community. The LXX renders οτι εγενηθη ενοχος (because he has become guilty), softening the terrifying identification of Israel with the cherem. The formal rendering preserves the MT's stark declaration.

12. **Verse 14 -- "which YHWH takes" (אֲשֶׁר יִלְכְּדֶנּוּ יְהוָה):** The verb לכד (to capture, take) is the same verb used for the capture of cities in warfare (cf. 6:20 for Jericho). The sacred lot "captures" the guilty party as a military operation captures an enemy. The means of selection -- whether Urim and Thummim, lots, or some other sacral mechanism -- is not specified. The LXX renders ον αν δειξη κυριος (whom the Lord shall designate), using a different verb that emphasizes divine indication rather than capture.

13. **Verse 15 -- "burned with fire" (יִשָּׂרֵף בָּאֵשׁ):** The punishment mirrors the fate prescribed for the cherem. The one who took devoted things becomes a devoted thing and must be consumed. The LXX adds και πάντα οσα εστιν αυτω (and all things that are his), expanding the scope explicitly. The relationship between burning and stoning in verses 15 and 25 has generated extensive discussion; both appear in the execution narrative, suggesting a compound punishment.

14. **Verse 15 -- "a disgraceful thing" (נְבָלָה):** The term nevalah denotes an outrageous act of moral folly that violates the norms of the covenant community. It is used for Shechem's rape of Dinah (Genesis 34:7), the crime at Gibeah (Judges 19:23-24; 20:6, 10), and Amnon's assault on Tamar (2 Samuel 13:12). The LXX renders ανομημα (a lawless act). The word carries a weight far beyond "foolishness"; it designates conduct that is both morally outrageous and socially destructive, a violation of the fundamental order of Israel.

15. **Verse 19 -- "give glory...give him a confession" (שִׂים...כָבוֹד...וְתֶן־לוֹ תוֹדָה):** The phrase "give glory to YHWH" is a solemn adjuration formula calling for truthful testimony (cf. John 9:24). The combination with "give a confession" (todah, from ידה) makes this a formal legal-religious demand: Achan is being adjured to acknowledge the truth before the divine judge. The LXX renders δος δοξαν...και δος εξομολογησιν. The courtroom and the sanctuary converge.

16. **Verse 21 -- "a beautiful mantle of Shinar" (אַדֶּרֶת שִׁנְעָר אַחַת טוֹבָה):** The addereth was a prestige garment, a cloak or mantle. "Shinar" is the biblical name for Babylonia (Genesis 10:10; 11:2; 14:1, 9; Daniel 1:2). A Babylonian garment among the spoils of a Canaanite city reflects the international trade networks of the Late Bronze Age. The LXX renders ψιλην ποικιλην καλην (a beautiful decorated garment), omitting the geographical reference to Shinar entirely. The formal rendering preserves the MT's "mantle of Shinar," which carries resonance with Babel/Babylon throughout the biblical narrative.

17. **Verse 21 -- "I coveted them" (וָאֶחְמְדֵם):** The verb חמד is the same verb used in the Tenth Commandment: "You shall not covet" (Exodus 20:17; Deuteronomy 5:21). Achan's confession follows the progression of sin: "I saw...I coveted...I took" -- echoing the pattern of Genesis 3:6 (Eve "saw that the tree was good...took"). The LXX renders ενεθυμηθην αυτα (I desired them). The verbal allusion to the Decalogue makes Achan's sin a paradigmatic covenant violation.

18. **Verse 24 -- "the Valley of Achor" (עֵמֶק עָכוֹר):** The name means "Valley of Trouble," from the root עכר, the same root used in Joshua's pronouncement in verse 25: "Why have you troubled us?" (מֶה עֲכַרְתָּנוּ). The valley is identified with the Buqei'ah, a small valley in the wilderness of Judah west of Qumran. In Hosea 2:17 [English 2:15], YHWH promises to transform the Valley of Achor into "a door of hope" -- a profound eschatological reversal of the judgment scene in this chapter. The LXX renders Εμεκαχωρ, transliterating the Hebrew.

19. **Verse 24 -- "his sons and his daughters":** The inclusion of Achan's family in the punishment raises acute questions about collective responsibility. Deuteronomy 24:16 prohibits executing children for their fathers' sins. Several explanations have been proposed: (a) the family was complicit (the stolen goods were hidden in the shared tent); (b) the cherem contagion extended to all who shared the contaminated space; (c) this reflects the corporate understanding of household solidarity in ancient Israel. The LXX includes the family. The text does not explicitly state that the children were killed, though verse 25 says "all Israel stoned him with stones" (singular) before shifting to "they burned them with fire and they stoned them with stones" (plural) -- a textual variation that may distinguish Achan's personal execution from the destruction of his property and livestock.

20. **Verse 25 -- "Why have you troubled us? YHWH will trouble you" (מֶה עֲכַרְתָּנוּ יַעְכֳּרְךָ יְהוָה):** A paronomastic judgment oracle: the root עכר appears twice, binding Achan's crime to his punishment through the very sound of the words. The wordplay extends to the valley's name (Achor) and, by assonance, to Achan's own name (especially in its variant form Achar). The LXX renders τι ωλεθρευσας ημας; εξολεθρευσαι σε κυριος (Why have you destroyed us? The Lord will destroy you), using a different root (ολεθρ-) but preserving the repetition.

21. **Verse 25 -- Singular and plural:** The verse contains a notable shift: "all Israel stoned him (אֹתוֹ) with stones" (singular), then "they burned them (אֹתָם) with fire and they stoned them (אֹתָם) with stones" (plural). The singular may refer to Achan alone; the plural may encompass his possessions and livestock, or may include his family. The LXX reads the plural throughout. The formal rendering preserves the MT's alternation without harmonization.

22. **Verse 26 -- "YHWH turned from the burning of his anger" (וַיָּשָׁב יְהוָה מֵחֲרוֹן אַפּוֹ):** The verb שׁוּב (to turn, return) with the noun חרון (burning) and אף (anger/nostril) forms a standard expression for the cessation of divine wrath (cf. Exodus 32:12; Numbers 25:4; Deuteronomy 13:17). The removal of the cherem contamination restores the covenantal relationship. The LXX renders και επαυσατο κυριος του θυμου της οργης (and the Lord ceased from the fury of his wrath). The chapter thus moves from divine anger (v. 1) to the extinguishing of that anger (v. 26), forming an inclusio of wrath and restoration.

### Structural Observations

- Joshua 7 is structured as a chiastic narrative of covenant rupture and restoration. The chapter opens with Israel under YHWH's anger (v. 1) and closes with that anger turned away (v. 26). Between these poles, the narrative moves through defeat (vv. 2-5), lament (vv. 6-9), divine accusation and instruction (vv. 10-15), identification of the guilty (vv. 16-18), confession (vv. 19-21), verification (vv. 22-23), and judgment (vv. 24-26).

- The theological architecture of the chapter rests on the concept of cherem (devoted thing), which appears in verses 1, 11, 12, 13, and 15. The cherem functions as a contagion: Achan's private act contaminates all Israel (v. 1 -- "the sons of Israel committed a trespass"), renders the entire nation unable to stand before enemies (v. 12), and transforms Israel itself into a cherem (v. 12). Only the surgical removal of the contamination can restore the covenantal relationship.

- The fivefold accusation of verse 11 (sinned, transgressed, taken, stolen, deceived) corresponds to a progressive intensification: from general covenant violation to specific acts of theft, deception, and concealment. The chain of vegam ("and also") creates a rhetorical effect of mounting outrage, as if YHWH is cataloguing each dimension of the offense.

- Achan's confession (vv. 20-21) deliberately echoes the pattern of the primordial fall: "I saw...I coveted...I took" mirrors "she saw...it was desirable...she took" (Genesis 3:6). The narrative presents Achan's sin as a recapitulation of the fundamental human pattern of disobedience: the eye desires, the heart covets, and the hand takes what has been forbidden.

- The wordplay between Achan/Achar, the verb 'akhar (to trouble), and the Valley of Achor binds the entire narrative together through sound. The guilty man's identity is inscribed in the landscape: the place of his judgment bears his crime as its permanent name. This etiological function (v. 26: "therefore the name of that place has been called the Valley of Achor unto this day") transforms a historical event into a perpetual witness.

- The contrast between Joshua 6 and Joshua 7 is stark and deliberate: the triumphant conquest of Jericho is immediately followed by humiliating defeat at Ai. The transition from chapter 6 to chapter 7 is marked by the adversative conjunction "but" (ו functioning as adversative in context), signaling that the narrative has turned. Israel's power was never autonomous -- it depended entirely on the covenantal relationship with YHWH, and one man's sacrilege could nullify the military capacity of the entire nation.
